title: Pixels, Channels & Color Depth
concept: A color pixel is really three numbers stacked together — one each for red, green, and blue — and with 8-bit depth, each of those numbers ranges from 0 to 255.

exercises.prompt: You learned a grayscale pixel is one number. How many numbers do you think a single COLOR pixel needs?

options.text: Three — one for red, one for green, one for blue
A hundred, one for every possible color

exercises.explain: Right! Color pixels usually store three numbers, one per color channel.

cards.title: Why does a color photo take up more room than a black-and-white one?
cards.body: A grayscale photo needs one number per pixel to say how bright it is. But a full-color photo needs to capture red, green, AND blue separately for every single pixel. That's three times the numbers — and it's exactly why color images are 'heavier' than grayscale ones.

cards.title: Channels stack red, green, and blue layers
cards.body: A color image is made of three channels: a red layer, a green layer, and a blue layer (RGB), stacked on top of each other like transparent sheets. Every pixel gets one value from each layer. Color depth (often 8-bit) tells you how many different values each channel can hold — with 8 bits, each channel ranges from 0 to 255.

cards.title: Like mixing three colored spotlights on one spot
cards.body: Imagine three spotlights — one red, one green, one blue — all aimed at the same spot on a wall, and you can dim each one independently from 'off' (0) to 'full blast' (255). Turn all three all the way up and you get white. Turn them all off and you get black. Every color you've ever seen on a screen is some mix of those three dimmer settings.

cards.title: Building the color orange from three numbers
cards.body: A bright orange pixel might be stored as red = 255, green = 140, blue = 0 — lots of red, a good amount of green, and no blue. Change those three numbers even slightly (255, 100, 0) and you get a darker, more red-leaning orange. Every color in a photo is just a different combination of these three 0-255 values.

cards.title: 8-bit doesn't mean 8 colors
cards.body: It's easy to assume '8-bit' means only 8 possible colors — but 8 bits per channel actually means 256 possible values (0 through 255) per channel. With three channels, that's 256 × 256 × 256, or about 16.7 million possible colors for a single pixel.

cards.title: One gray number vs. three color numbers
cards.body: Same pixel position, very different storage needs.

right.text:
Three channels.
Three numbers, each 0–255.
Describes red, green, and blue mixed together.

exercises.prompt: In a standard RGB color image, how many channels does each pixel have?
exercises.explain: Correct. RGB stands for red, green, blue — three channels per pixel.

exercises.prompt: True or false: With 8-bit color depth, each channel can hold a value from 0 to 255.
exercises.explain: True. 8 bits gives 256 possible values per channel, numbered 0 through 255.

exercises.sentence: Mixing red, green, and blue channels all the way up to their maximum value produces the color ___.
exercises.explain: White is correct. Full red + full green + full blue combine to make white light.

exercises.prompt: A student claims: 'A color pixel with values (255, 0, 0) is mostly blue.' Is this reasoning sound?

options.text: No — in RGB order, (255, 0, 0) means full red, no green, no blue, so it's a pure red pixel
Yes, the last number always controls the dominant color
Yes, because 255 is a 'cool' number associated with blue

exercises.explain: Right. In RGB order the first number is red, so (255, 0, 0) is pure red, not blue.

exercises.prompt: A pixel is stored as (30, 30, 30) in an 8-bit RGB image. What does this tell you?

options.text: It's a dark, near-black gray, since red, green, and blue are all low and roughly equal
It's a bright, vivid green pixel
It must be an error, since RGB values can't repeat
It's pure white, since all three numbers match

exercises.explain: Exactly. Low and roughly equal RGB values produce a dark gray — equal values always sit somewhere on the black-to-white scale.
